evals.name: microservice-libs
evals.prompt:
I'm starting a new payments microservice and need to lock in 5 library/tooling decisions before I scaffold the repo. Help me compare and pick. I want to see the trade-offs visually instead of just reading a list. Decisions:

1. HTTP client — (a) requests + manual retry (recommended, 0 new deps, ~30 lines retry helper), (b) httpx with built-in retries (1 new dep, async-ready)
2. Logging format — (a) plain text (0 cost), (b) structured JSON via structlog (recommended, 1 dep + log shipper config)
3. Test framework — (a) unittest stdlib (0 deps), (b) pytest (recommended, 1 dev dep, fixtures + parametrize)
4. Dependency manager — (a) poetry (mature, slow), (b) uv (recommended, ~10x faster), (c) pip-tools (minimal)
5. Containerization — (a) Dockerfile (recommended, standard), (b) nix flake (reproducible, steeper), (c) none — local-only for now

End with a markdown summary of what I picked.
evals.expected_output: Some interactive way to step through the 5 decisions (HTML wizard preferred), plus a final markdown summary of choices.

assertions.text: summary.md exists in the output directory
All 5 decisions are referenced in produced artifacts (HTTP client, Logging, Test framework, Dependency manager, Containerization)
Each decision lists at least 2 options with labels
Recommended option is flagged for each decision (matches the prompt's recommendations)
Output explains how the user communicates their picks back
An HTML file is produced that lets the user compare options visually
Produced HTML has no references to external script/style URLs (self-contained)

evals.name: cli-writing-audit
evals.prompt:
We did a UX writing audit on our CLI tool and found 8 inconsistencies that each need a call. I want to step through them, see the current vs proposed for each, leave notes where I'm unsure, and end up with a structured result I can hand to whoever's doing the code edits.

1. Pager format — (a) [n/n] recommended (~4 lines, matches majority), (b) n-n of n (~6 lines), (c) Showing n-n of n (verbose, dialog-only)
2. Size unit spacing — (a) '5 MB' SI style, (b) '5MB' compact, (c) leave as-is (recommended; SI for size, compact for age is convention)
3. Error prefix — (a) 'error:' lowercase (recommended, matches Go convention), (b) 'Error:' capitalized, (c) mix by severity
4. Sentence punctuation — (a) labels no period, full sentences yes (recommended), (b) always period, (c) never period
5. Truncate suffix — (a) '...' three chars (recommended, consistent), (b) '..' two chars (current in some places)
6. Empty / scanning state — (a) policy: '-' before scan, '0 B' if zero, '(empty)' for empty containers (recommended), (b) keep current 5-way mix
7. Safety dot accessibility — (a) shape + color (●/◐/◆) (recommended, ~8% colorblind users), (b) color only
8. Footer verb casing — (a) lowercase ('up', 'select') (recommended, majority pattern), (b) Title Case

Give me something I can click through and export.
evals.expected_output: Interactive HTML or equivalent that lets the user pick per decision with the option to leave notes; export as markdown or JSON at the end.

assertions.text: summary.md exists in the output directory
All 8 decisions are referenced in produced artifacts
Each of the 8 decisions presents both the current state and the proposed options
Output supports per-decision notes (textarea or equivalent)
An HTML file is produced that the user can click through
Produced HTML has no references to external script/style URLs (self-contained)
Output explains how the user exports their picks (markdown or JSON)

evals.name: small-design-set
evals.prompt:
Quick one — I have 4 small design decisions from a meeting and just want to think them over briefly before picking. Nothing fancy needed.

1. Primary button — (a) flat (recommended, matches design system), (b) raised (older style)
2. Empty state copy — (a) 'No items yet' (recommended), (b) 'Nothing here'
3. Loading indicator — (a) spinner (recommended, simple), (b) skeleton screen (more work)
4. Form layout — (a) single column (recommended, mobile-friendly), (b) two columns (faster to scan on desktop)

What do you suggest?
evals.expected_output: Inline answer with brief comparison and a recommended pick per item. The skill should NOT be invoked for only 4 decisions — its SKILL.md states a 5+ threshold. Tests appropriate non-invocation.

assertions.text: All 4 decisions get a direct recommendation in summary.md
No HTML wizard is generated (the skill's threshold is 5+ items, this is 4)
If a wizard was generated anyway, the agent explained why it overrode the threshold
